schools.summary: The Table of Nations heading: 'now these are the generations of the sons of Noah, Shem, Ham, and Japheth: and unto them were sons born after the flood' — the formula 'these are the generations (toledoth) of the sons of Noah' introducing the genealogy of the nations (the Table of Nations, ch. 10, the chapter tracing the descendants of Shem, Ham, and Japheth, and the peoples that sprang from them, the record of the origin of all the nations from Noah's three sons); the three sons of Noah the heads of the post-flood race (from them the whole earth peopled, 9:19, the genealogy showing how the nations descended from the three and spread over the earth, the unity of mankind in their descent from Noah, and the diversity of the nations); 'unto them were sons born after the flood' (the descendants of the three sons born after the flood, in the renewed world, the multiplying of mankind, 9:1, 7, issuing in the nations of the Table, the peopling of the earth from Noah's family, the Table recording the fulfillment of the command to be fruitful and replenish the earth) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Henry, Calvin.

themes: Now these are the generations of the sons of Noah, Shem, Ham, and Japheth: and unto them were sons born after the flood
the heading of the generations of Noah's sons (the Table of Nations)
the origin of all the nations from Noah's three sons
the unity of mankind and the peopling of the renewed earth

schools.summary: The sons of Japheth: 'the sons of Japheth; Gomer, and Magog, and Madai, and Javan, and Tubal, and Meshech, and Tiras' — the seven sons of Japheth named first (from them descending the peoples of the north and west, Europe and northern Asia, the genealogy beginning with Japheth, the most widely spread, 9:27); the peoples descended from Japheth's sons (as identified by the ancient writers): Gomer (the Cimmerians, and the Celtic/Galatian peoples); Magog (the Scythians); Madai (the Medes); Javan (the Ionians/Greeks); Tubal and Meshech (peoples of Asia Minor, the Tibareni and Moschi); Tiras (the Thracians); the heads of the northern and western nations; from these seven sons the great body of the Gentile nations of Europe and the north (the enlargement of Japheth, 9:27, seen in the wide spread of his descendants, the peoples of the isles of the Gentiles, v.5, the origin of the northern and western nations traced to Japheth) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Clarke, Gill.

themes: The sons of Japheth; Gomer, and Magog, and Madai, and Javan, and Tubal, and Meshech, and Tiras
the sons of Japheth, the heads of the northern and western nations
Gomer (Cimmerians/Celts), Magog (Scythians), Madai (Medes), Javan (Greeks)
the wide spread of Japheth's line (9:27)

schools.summary: The sons of Gomer: 'and the sons of Gomer; Ashkenaz, and Riphath, and Togarmah' — the three sons of Gomer (himself a son of Japheth, v.2, from them descending further peoples of the north and Asia Minor, the genealogy tracing the second generation from Japheth through Gomer); the peoples identified with Gomer's sons (by the ancient writers): Ashkenaz (a people of the north, associated with the region near Armenia, and later with the Germanic/northern peoples); Riphath (a northern people, perhaps near the Riphaean mountains); Togarmah (the Armenians, the house of Togarmah, a people of the north, Ezekiel 27:14; 38:6); from Gomer's sons peoples of the north and Asia Minor (the genealogy showing the multiplying and spreading of Japheth's descendants into many nations, the peoples of the north traced through Gomer, the increase of the Gentile nations) — Gill, Ellicott, Clarke.

themes: And the sons of Gomer; Ashkenaz, and Riphath, and Togarmah
the sons of Gomer, the further peoples of the north
Ashkenaz, Riphath, and Togarmah (the Armenians and northern peoples)
the further spread of Japheth's line

schools.summary: The sons of Javan: 'and the sons of Javan; Elishah, and Tarshish, Kittim, and Dodanim' — the four sons of Javan (the head of the Greeks/Ionians, v.2, from them descending the maritime and western peoples, those of the isles and coasts, the genealogy tracing the seafaring nations through Javan); the peoples identified with Javan's sons: Elishah (a people of Greece, perhaps Hellas/the Aeolians, or the coasts of the Aegean, Ezekiel 27:7); Tarshish (a maritime people, often identified with Tartessus in Spain, or a distant seaport, the ships of Tarshish); Kittim (the people of Cyprus, and the western isles/coasts, later the Romans/Macedonians); Dodanim (or Rodanim, the people of Rhodes, or a kindred Greek people); from Javan's sons the peoples of the isles and coasts (v.5), the seafaring nations of the west, the spread of Japheth's line over the islands and coastlands, the maritime peoples of the Mediterranean traced to Javan — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Clarke, Gill.

themes: And the sons of Javan; Elishah, and Tarshish, Kittim, and Dodanim
the sons of Javan, the heads of the maritime peoples
Elishah, Tarshish, Kittim (Cyprus), Dodanim (Rhodes)
the seafaring peoples of the isles and coasts

schools.summary: The isles of the Gentiles divided: 'by these were the isles of the Gentiles divided in their lands; every one after his tongue, after their families, in their nations' — by the descendants of Japheth (vv.2-4) the 'isles of the Gentiles' (the coastlands and islands, especially of the Mediterranean and the west) divided and settled (the peoples of Japheth spreading over the isles and coasts, the term 'isles' embracing the maritime regions peopled by Japheth's line); the division 'every one after his tongue' (the nations distinguished by their languages, the diversity of tongues, anticipating, or referring to, the confusion at Babel, 11:7-9, marking the separation of the peoples, each nation having its own tongue); 'after their families, in their nations' (the peoples divided by families, lands, tongues, and nations, the orderly spread and settlement of the nations, God's providence in dividing the earth among the peoples, Deuteronomy 32:8, 'when the Most High divided to the nations their inheritance,' the formula, repeated for Ham, v.20, and Shem, v.31, marking the ordered distribution of the nations) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Wesley, Gill, JFB.

themes: By these were the isles of the Gentiles divided in their lands; every one after his tongue, after their families, in their nations
the division of the isles of the Gentiles among Japheth's descendants
the nations distinguished by their tongues, families, and lands
the earth divided among the nations by God's providence (Deuteronomy 32:8)

schools.summary: The sons of Ham: 'and the sons of Ham; Cush, and Mizraim, and Phut, and Canaan' — the four sons of Ham (from them descending the peoples of the south, Africa, and parts of Arabia and Canaan, the genealogy tracing the southern nations through Ham); the peoples identified with Ham's sons: Cush (Ethiopia, and parts of Arabia, the Cushites); Mizraim (Egypt, the name the regular Hebrew word for Egypt); Phut (Libya, the Libyans of north Africa); Canaan (the Canaanites, the peoples of the land of Canaan); among Ham's sons Canaan, whose descendants (vv.15-19) peopled the land of Canaan, on whom the curse fell (9:25, the note of Canaan, as in 9:18, connecting the genealogy with the prophecy, the southern nations and the Canaanites traced to Ham, the peoples of Egypt, Ethiopia, Libya, and Canaan from Ham's line) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Clarke, Gill, Henry.

themes: And the sons of Ham; Cush, and Mizraim, and Phut, and Canaan
the sons of Ham, the heads of the southern nations
Cush (Ethiopia), Mizraim (Egypt), Phut (Libya), Canaan (the Canaanites)
Canaan, on whom the curse fell (9:25), among Ham's sons

schools.summary: The sons of Cush: 'and the sons of Cush; Seba, and Havilah, and Sabtah, and Raamah, and Sabtecha: and the sons of Raamah; Sheba, and Dedan' — the five sons of Cush (and two grandsons through Raamah, from them descending peoples of Arabia, the Red Sea region, and parts of Africa, the genealogy tracing the Cushite peoples); the peoples identified with Cush's sons: Seba (a people of Africa/Arabia, Meroe or the Sabeans); Havilah (a region of Arabia); Sabtah, Sabtecha (peoples of Arabia); Raamah (a people of Arabia), and his sons Sheba and Dedan (Arabian peoples, the Sabeans and Dedanites, trading peoples of Arabia, Ezekiel 27:20-22); from Cush's sons peoples of Arabia and the south (the Cushite line spreading over Arabia and the regions of the Red Sea, the multiplying of Ham's descendants, the peoples of the south and Arabia traced through Cush) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And the sons of Cush; Seba, and Havilah, and Sabtah, and Raamah, and Sabtecha: and the sons of Raamah; Sheba, and Dedan
the sons of Cush, the peoples of Arabia and the south
Seba, Havilah, Raamah, Sheba, and Dedan (Arabian peoples)
the Cushite line spread over Arabia and the Red Sea

schools.summary: Nimrod the first mighty one: 'and Cush begat Nimrod: he began to be a mighty one in the earth' — Nimrod, a son (or descendant) of Cush, singled out from the genealogy for special notice (the notable figure of the Table of Nations, the narrative pausing on Nimrod, the founder of the first kingdom, v.10); 'he began to be a mighty one (a gibbor, a mighty man, a hero) in the earth' (the first to rise to great power and dominion, beginning the era of mighty men, conquerors, and rulers, 'began to be' marking him as the first potentate, the founder of empire, whether by force, prowess, or tyranny, Nimrod making himself mighty and founding a kingdom, v.10); Nimrod's might (whether admired or condemned, see v.9) marking the rise of human power and dominion after the flood (he gathering men under his rule and building the first kingdom, Babel and the cities of Shinar, v.10, the beginning of organized human empire, and, on the dominant reading, of tyranny and despotism, the first mighty one of the post-flood world) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Henry, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Cush begat Nimrod: he began to be a mighty one in the earth
Nimrod, who began to be a mighty one in the earth (the first potentate)
the first to rise to great power and dominion, the founder of empire
the rise of human power and the first kingdom (v.10)

schools.summary: Gill, the grammatical-historical, and the ancient/Jewish tradition (the bad sense): 'he was a mighty hunter before the LORD' taken in a bad sense — Nimrod a 'mighty hunter' not merely of beasts but of men, a violent conqueror, a tyrant who hunted and subdued men, seizing dominion by force; 'before the LORD' read as 'against the LORD' or 'in defiance of the LORD' (the name Nimrod, from marad, 'he rebelled,' the Targums and Jewish tradition branding him as a rebel, apostate, and tyrant, the Jerusalem Targum and the rabbis making him the arch-rebel who founded a kingdom in defiance of God); 'before the LORD' meaning either 'in the LORD's sight,' the superlative idiom, exceedingly mighty, or 'against the LORD,' in open defiance, the dominant view, with the name and the Jewish tradition, taking it as marking Nimrod's God-defying tyranny; 'Even as Nimrod the mighty hunter before the LORD' (Nimrod becoming proverbial, a byword for a mighty, masterful, and commonly a tyrannical hunter and ruler, his name passing into a proverb for any powerful tyrant).

A milder reading takes 'mighty hunter before the LORD' in a more neutral or even favorable sense — a literal hunter of beasts (protecting men from wild animals), or one eminently mighty 'in the sight of the LORD' (an idiom for exceeding greatness), without necessarily the note of rebellion; 'before the LORD' read as 'in the LORD's sight' (the superlative idiom) rather than 'against the LORD.' The sense of the phrase divides between the tyrant/rebel reading (the dominant) and the literal-hunter or eminent-might reading.

debates.question: Is Nimrod 'a mighty hunter before the LORD' in a bad sense (tyrant/rebel) or a neutral one?

sides.position: A tyrant and rebel who hunted men in defiance of God ('before' = 'against the LORD'; from marad, 'he rebelled'; the dominant Jewish/traditional reading)
A literal hunter of beasts, or one eminently mighty 'in the sight of the LORD' (the milder, neutral reading)

themes: He was a mighty hunter before the LORD: wherefore it is said, Even as Nimrod the mighty hunter before the LORD
Nimrod the mighty hunter before the LORD (the crux of the phrase)
a tyrant and rebel hunting men in defiance of God vs a literal hunter or one eminently mighty
Nimrod's name a byword for any powerful tyrant

schools.summary: Nimrod's kingdom in Shinar: 'and the beginning of his kingdom was Babel, and Erech, and Accad, and Calneh, in the land of Shinar' — Nimrod founding the first kingdom, and its beginning being Babel (Babylon, the first organized empire of the post-flood world, Babel, the city later associated with the tower and the confusion of tongues, ch. 11, the seat of Nimrod's rule, the first kingdom of human dominion); Nimrod's kingdom comprising four cities — Babel, Erech, Accad, and Calneh (the chief cities of his realm in the plain of Shinar, Babylonia/Mesopotamia, the centers of the first empire, the cities later known in the history of Babylonia); 'in the land of Shinar' (the plain of Shinar, Babylonia, lower Mesopotamia, where the first kingdom was founded and where the tower of Babel would be built, 11:2, the fertile plain that became the seat of the first empire, Nimrod's kingdom established in Shinar marking the beginning of empire and the place of the dispersion) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And the beginning of his kingdom was Babel, and Erech, and Accad, and Calneh, in the land of Shinar
the beginning of Nimrod's kingdom in the land of Shinar
the first organized empire, seated in the plain of Babylonia
Babel, where the tower would rise (11:2)

schools.summary: The building of Nineveh: 'out of that land went forth Asshur, and builded Nineveh, and the city Rehoboth, and Calah' — Keil and Delitzsch and many holding the verse should read 'out of that land he [Nimrod] went forth to Asshur (Assyria)' — Nimrod, from Shinar, extending his kingdom northward into Assyria and building Nineveh and the other cities, 'Asshur' taken as the place (Assyria) to which Nimrod went, not the person, the Targum of Jonathan and several reading it of Nimrod: 'out of that land went forth Nimrod and reigned... and built Nineveh'; a second reading (the KJV text) taking 'Asshur' as the person — Asshur (the son of Shem, v.22), going out of Shinar, building Nineveh and the cities of Assyria, on this view Shem's son Asshur, not Nimrod, founding Nineveh; Nineveh (the great city, later the capital of Assyria) and the cities Rehoboth and Calah built (the founding of the great cities of Assyria, whether by Nimrod, extending his empire, or by Asshur, founding his own, the cities of Assyria established, Nineveh becoming the famous capital of the Assyrian empire, Jonah; Nahum) — Keil and Delitzsch, JFB, Gill.

debates.question: Did Nimrod go forth to Asshur (Assyria), or did Asshur (son of Shem) go forth, to build Nineveh?

sides.position: 'Out of that land he [Nimrod] went forth to Asshur (Assyria)' and built Nineveh — Nimrod extending his kingdom northward (the margin; Keil; the Targum)
'Out of that land went forth Asshur' (the son of Shem) and built Nineveh — Asshur founding the cities of Assyria (the KJV text)

themes: Out of that land went forth Asshur, and builded Nineveh, and the city Rehoboth, and Calah
the building of Nineveh (the crux of whether Asshur or Nimrod went forth)
Nimrod extending his kingdom to Assyria vs Asshur founding the cities
the founding of the great cities of Assyria

schools.summary: Resen, the great city: 'and Resen between Nineveh and Calah: the same is a great city' — Resen, a city lying between Nineveh and Calah, completing the list of the cities of Assyria (vv.11-12, 'the same is a great city,' the description, whether of Resen, or of the whole complex of Nineveh, Rehoboth, Calah, and Resen taken together as the 'great city,' marking the greatness of the Assyrian metropolis); Resen lying between Nineveh and Calah (the cluster of cities, Nineveh, Rehoboth, Calah, Resen, forming the great urban complex of Assyria, the chief cities of the Assyrian heartland); 'the same is a great city' (whether Resen itself, or the whole cluster taken as one great city, the 'great city' of Nineveh, embracing the surrounding towns, perhaps the 'great city' of Jonah 3:3, the greatness of the Assyrian metropolis noted, the rise of the great cities of empire after the flood, the urban centers of the first great kingdoms) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Resen between Nineveh and Calah: the same is a great city
Resen, completing the cities of Assyria
the cluster of cities forming the great Assyrian metropolis (Jonah 3:3)
the rise of the great cities of empire after the flood

schools.summary: The sons of Mizraim: 'and Mizraim begat Ludim, and Anamim, and Lehabim, and Naphtuhim' — the peoples (named in the plural, as tribes/nations) descended from Mizraim (Egypt, v.6, from Mizraim coming various peoples of Egypt and north Africa, the genealogy tracing the Egyptian and Libyan peoples through Mizraim); the names (in the plural form) denoting peoples or tribes: Ludim (a people, perhaps of north Africa or the Lydians of Egypt); Anamim, Lehabim (the Lehabim perhaps the Libyans), Naphtuhim (a people of Egypt); the tribes and peoples descended from Mizraim, settled in Egypt and north Africa; from Mizraim the peoples of Egypt and the neighboring regions (the Egyptian nation and its kindred peoples traced to Ham through Mizraim, the multiplying of Ham's line in the south) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And Mizraim begat Ludim, and Anamim, and Lehabim, and Naphtuhim
the sons of Mizraim, the peoples of Egypt and north Africa
Ludim, Anamim, Lehabim (Libyans), and Naphtuhim
the peoples of Egypt and Libya traced to Ham through Mizraim

schools.summary: Further peoples of Mizraim and the Philistines: 'and Pathrusim, and Casluhim, (out of whom came Philistim,) and Caphtorim' — further peoples descended from Mizraim: Pathrusim (the people of Pathros, Upper Egypt); Casluhim and Caphtorim (peoples of Egypt or the neighboring coasts); the note '(out of whom came Philistim)' marking the origin of the Philistines from one of these peoples; the Philistines (Philistim) springing from the Casluhim (or, by another reckoning, connected with the Caphtorim, Caphtor; cf. Amos 9:7; Jeremiah 47:4, the origin of the Philistines, the later great enemies of Israel, here traced to the line of Ham through Mizraim, the Philistines, a people of the Egyptian/Caphtorite stock, settling on the coast of Canaan); the Caphtorim (the people of Caphtor, perhaps Crete or the Egyptian coast) connected with the origin of the Philistines (Deuteronomy 2:23; Amos 9:7) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And Pathrusim, and Casluhim, (out of whom came Philistim,) and Caphtorim
further peoples of Mizraim, including the origin of the Philistines
the Philistines (Israel's later foes) traced to Ham through Mizraim (Amos 9:7)
the peoples of Egypt and the coast

schools.summary: The sons of Canaan: 'and Canaan begat Sidon his firstborn, and Heth' — Canaan (the son of Ham, v.6, on whom the curse fell, 9:25) begetting Sidon, his firstborn (Sidon, the head of the Sidonians/Phoenicians, the founder of the great maritime city of Sidon, the firstborn of the Canaanite line); Sidon, the firstborn, the head of the Sidonians/Phoenicians, the great traders and seafarers of the Canaanite coast (the city of Sidon, and Tyre, her daughter, becoming famous, the Phoenician peoples descended from Canaan's firstborn); 'and Heth' (Heth, the head of the Hittites, the children of Heth, the Hittites of the land of Canaan, with whom Abraham later dealt, ch. 23, the Hittites a great Canaanite people, the heads of the Canaanite nations beginning with Sidon and Heth, the peoples on whom the curse, 9:25, would fall, and whom Israel would later dispossess) — Gill, Clarke, Henry.

themes: And Canaan begat Sidon his firstborn, and Heth
the sons of Canaan, the heads of the Canaanite peoples
Sidon (the Phoenicians) and Heth (the Hittites)
the peoples on whom the curse fell (9:25), whom Israel would dispossess

schools.summary: Further Canaanite peoples: 'and the Jebusite, and the Amorite, and the Girgasite' — further Canaanite peoples descended from Canaan: the Jebusites (the people of Jebus, later Jerusalem); the Amorites (a great Canaanite people of the hill country and beyond Jordan); the Girgashites (a Canaanite people); the tribes of the land of Canaan, listed among the seven (or more) nations Israel would dispossess; the Jebusites holding Jebus (Jerusalem) until David took it (2 Samuel 5), the Amorites a powerful people (Sihon and Og, Amorite kings), the Girgashites a Canaanite tribe (the peoples named the Canaanite nations of the land); the Jebusites, Amorites, and Girgashites among the Canaanite nations (the 'seven nations,' Deuteronomy 7:1) that inhabited the land before Israel, the peoples of the cursed line of Canaan (9:25), the nations Israel would later dispossess in the conquest — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And the Jebusite, and the Amorite, and the Girgasite
further Canaanite peoples descended from Canaan
the Jebusites (Jerusalem), Amorites (the hill country), and Girgashites
the Canaanite nations (Deuteronomy 7:1) Israel would dispossess

schools.summary: Further Canaanite peoples: 'and the Hivite, and the Arkite, and the Sinite' — further peoples descended from Canaan: the Hivites (a Canaanite people, the Gibeonites being Hivites, Joshua 9); the Arkites (the people of Arka, a city of Phoenicia, north of Sidon); the Sinites (a people of the Phoenician coast or Lebanon); the Canaanite and Phoenician tribes of the north; the Hivites a Canaanite people (the Gibeonites, Joshua 9; and the Shechemites, ch. 34), the Arkites and Sinites northern Phoenician peoples, named from their cities (the tribes of the Canaanite north); the Hivites, Arkites, and Sinites, with the others, making up the many tribes of Canaan and Phoenicia (the Canaanite peoples spread along the coast and inland, the descendants of Canaan filling the land that would become the inheritance of Israel, the many nations of the cursed line) — Gill, JFB.

themes: And the Hivite, and the Arkite, and the Sinite
further Canaanite peoples and northern tribes
the Hivites (Gibeonites), Arkites, and Sinites (northern Phoenicians)
the many peoples descended from Canaan, filling the land

schools.summary: The last Canaanite tribes: 'and the Arvadite, and the Zemarite, and the Hamathite: and afterward were the families of the Canaanites spread abroad' — the last of the Canaanite peoples named: the Arvadites (the people of Arvad, a Phoenician island-city); the Zemarites (a people of the Phoenician coast); the Hamathites (the people of Hamath, a city of Syria on the Orontes); the northern Canaanite and Syrian peoples, completing the list of Canaan's descendants; these northern Phoenician and Syrian peoples, named from their cities (Arvad, Zemar, Hamath), the tribes of the Canaanite and Syrian north, the completing of the eleven peoples descended from Canaan; 'afterward were the families of the Canaanites spread abroad' (the Canaanite families spreading out over the land, and beyond, settling the regions described in v.19, the dispersion and settlement of the many Canaanite peoples, the spreading of Canaan's line over the land that would become Israel's, the increase and distribution of the cursed line) — Gill.

themes: And the Arvadite, and the Zemarite, and the Hamathite: and afterward were the families of the Canaanites spread abroad
the last Canaanite tribes and their spreading abroad
the Arvadites, Zemarites, and Hamathites (northern Phoenicians and Syrians)
the many families of Canaan settling over the land

schools.summary: The borders of the Canaanites: 'and the border of the Canaanites was from Sidon, as thou comest to Gerar, unto Gaza; as thou goest, unto Sodom, and Gomorrah, and Admah, and Zeboim, even unto Lasha' — the territory of the Canaanites described by its borders: from Sidon (in the north) toward Gerar and Gaza (in the south-west), and toward Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, and Zeboim (the cities of the plain, in the south-east) unto Lasha (the extent of the land the Canaanites occupied, the land that would become the promised land of Israel); the line from Sidon in the north down the coast toward Gerar and Gaza (the western and southern extent of the Canaanite territory along the coast and the south); the eastern/southern border running toward the cities of the plain (Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboim, later destroyed, ch. 19, the land of Canaan, bounded by these cities, the land promised to Abraham, 12:7; 15:18-21, the borders marking the inheritance Israel would receive, the territory of the cursed line that would pass to the chosen people) — Gill.

themes: And the border of the Canaanites was from Sidon... unto Sodom, and Gomorrah, and Admah, and Zeboim, even unto Lasha
the borders of the land of the Canaanites
the extent of the Canaanite territory, from Sidon to the cities of the plain
the land promised to Abraham (15:18-21) and the inheritance of Israel

schools.summary: Summary of the sons of Ham: 'these are the sons of Ham, after their families, after their tongues, in their countries, and in their nations' — the summary formula (as for Japheth, v.5, and Shem, v.31) closing the genealogy of Ham (the descendants of Ham distinguished by their families, tongues, countries, and nations, the ordered distribution of the Hamite peoples over the earth); the peoples divided by families and tongues (each nation with its own kindred and language, the diversity of tongues, anticipating, or referring to, Babel, 11:7-9, marking the separation of the Hamite nations, the ordered distinction of the peoples); 'in their countries, and in their nations' (the Hamite peoples settling in their several countries and forming their nations, the southern nations — Egypt, Ethiopia, Libya, Canaan, and the Cushite peoples — distributed over their lands, God's providence in settling the nations in their territories, Deuteronomy 32:8; Acts 17:26, the ordered spread of Ham's line) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: These are the sons of Ham, after their families, after their tongues, in their countries, and in their nations
the summary of the sons of Ham, in their families, tongues, and nations
the southern nations distinguished by kindred, language, and land
settled by God's providence in their territories (Acts 17:26)

schools.summary: Shem, the father of Eber: 'unto Shem also, the father of all the children of Eber, the brother of Japheth the elder, even to him were children born' — Shem named as the ancestor of Eber (from whom the Hebrews are named, and through whom the line of promise runs to Abraham, the genealogy of Shem given last and with special honor, because the line of promise, and the Messiah, descends through Shem and Eber, Shem the father of the chosen line); Eber (from whom 'Hebrew' is derived) singled out — Shem 'the father of all the children of Eber,' the ancestor of the Hebrew people and the line of Abraham (the special notice of Eber marking the line of promise, the honor of Shem's genealogy that it leads to the chosen people); 'the brother of Japheth the elder' variously rendered (it may mean that Japheth was the elder brother, Japheth the eldest of Noah's sons, or that Shem was the elder, most taking Japheth as the elder, Shem being named first for the honor of the line of promise, not for primogeniture, the order of the genealogy following the line of promise, not birth order) — Gill, Calvin, Henry, Clarke.

themes: Unto Shem also, the father of all the children of Eber, the brother of Japheth the elder, even to him were children born
Shem, the father of Eber's children and the line of promise
the ancestor of the Hebrews, through whom the line runs to Abraham
Shem named with special honor for the line of promise

schools.summary: The sons of Shem: 'the children of Shem; Elam, and Asshur, and Arphaxad, and Lud, and Aram' — the five sons of Shem (from them descending the peoples of the east and the line of promise, the genealogy tracing the Semitic nations through Shem); the peoples identified with Shem's sons: Elam (the Elamites, the people of Elam/Persia, east of Babylonia); Asshur (the Assyrians, the people of Assyria, though see v.11 on Nineveh); Arphaxad (the ancestor of the line of promise, through Eber to Abraham, v.24); Lud (the Lydians, or a people of the east); Aram (the Aramaeans/Syrians); the heads of the eastern and Semitic nations; among Shem's sons Arphaxad, through whom the line of promise descends (to Eber, Peleg, and Abraham, v.24, the Semitic peoples — Elamites, Assyrians, Lydians, Aramaeans — spreading over the east, the line of the chosen people traced through Arphaxad) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke.

themes: The children of Shem; Elam, and Asshur, and Arphaxad, and Lud, and Aram
the sons of Shem, the heads of the eastern nations
Elam (Persia), Asshur (Assyria), Arphaxad (the line of promise), Lud, Aram (Syria)
Arphaxad, through whom the line of promise runs to Abraham

schools.summary: The sons of Aram: 'and the children of Aram; Uz, and Hul, and Gether, and Mash' — the four sons of Aram (the head of the Aramaeans/Syrians, v.22, from them descending Aramaean peoples of Syria and the regions to the east, the genealogy tracing the Aramaean tribes through Aram); the peoples identified with Aram's sons: Uz (the land of Uz, the home of Job, Job 1:1, a region of the Aramaean/Arabian borderlands); Hul, Gether, and Mash (Aramaean peoples of Syria and the eastern regions, variously located); the Aramaean tribes of the north and east; from Aram's sons the Aramaean peoples of Syria and the borderlands (the Aramaean line spread over Syria and the regions east of Canaan, the Semitic peoples of the north traced through Aram, the kindred of the line of promise, the Aramaeans being close kin to the line of Abraham, who came from Aram, Deuteronomy 26:5) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And the children of Aram; Uz, and Hul, and Gether, and Mash
the sons of Aram, the Aramaean peoples
Uz (Job's land), Hul, Gether, and Mash (Aramaean tribes of Syria)
the Aramaeans, kin to the line of promise (Deuteronomy 26:5)

schools.summary: The line of promise: 'and Arphaxad begat Salah; and Salah begat Eber' — the genealogy now following the line of promise: Arphaxad (son of Shem, v.22) begetting Salah, and Salah begetting Eber (the direct line from Shem toward Abraham, the chosen line traced through these names, the genealogy narrowing from the nations to the line of the promised seed); the line running Arphaxad to Salah to Eber (the genealogy of the line of promise, continued in 11:10-26 to Abraham, the careful tracing of the chosen line through which the Messiah would come); 'Salah begat Eber' (Eber, from whom the Hebrews are named, v.21, and the ancestor of Peleg, v.25, and Abraham, the line of promise passing through Eber, the genealogy fixing on Eber as the head of the Hebrew line, the chosen line carried from Shem through Arphaxad, Salah, and Eber toward Abraham) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And Arphaxad begat Salah; and Salah begat Eber
the line of promise through Arphaxad, Salah, and Eber
the direct line from Shem toward Abraham
the genealogy narrowing from the nations to the line of the promised seed

schools.summary: Peleg and the division of the earth: 'and unto Eber were born two sons: the name of one was Peleg; for in his days was the earth divided; and his brother's name was Joktan' — Eber having two sons, Peleg (through whom the line of promise continues to Abraham, 11:16-26) and Joktan (the ancestor of various Arabian peoples, vv.26-29, the genealogy dividing here into the line of Peleg, the promise, and the line of Joktan, the Arabian tribes); Ellicott and the dominant reading taking 'the earth divided' as the division of the earth among the nations — the dispersion of mankind at Babel (11:1-9), the confusion of tongues and the scattering of the peoples into separate nations, which happened in Peleg's days (his name, Peleg, meaning 'division,' commemorating the event), a political/national division of the earth among the peoples; a second reading (Clarke) taking it as a physical division — the separation of continents and islands from the mainland (a literal geographical or geological division of the earth's surface) in Peleg's days, on this view a physical, not a political, division; Joktan, the brother of Peleg, the ancestor of many Arabian tribes (vv.26-29, the line of Joktan peopling Arabia, while the line of Peleg carried the promise) — Gill, Ellicott, Clarke.

debates.question: What is meant by 'in his days was the earth divided'?

sides.position: A political/national division — the dispersion of the nations at Babel and the confusion of tongues (11:1-9), in Peleg's days (the dominant reading)
A physical division — the separation of continents and islands from the mainland (a literal geographical division)

themes: And unto Eber were born two sons: the name of one was Peleg; for in his days was the earth divided; and his brother's name was Joktan
Peleg, in whose days the earth was divided (the crux of the division)
the political division of the nations at Babel vs a physical division of continents
Joktan heading the Arabian line, Peleg carrying the promise

schools.summary: The sons of Joktan: 'and Joktan begat Almodad, and Sheleph, and Hazarmaveth, and Jerah' — the sons of Joktan (the brother of Peleg, v.25), from whom descended many of the Arabian tribes (the genealogy tracing the peoples of Arabia through Joktan, the first of the thirteen sons of Joktan named, vv.26-29); the peoples and places identified with Joktan's sons: Almodad, Sheleph (Arabian peoples); Hazarmaveth (the region of Hadramaut, in southern Arabia); Jerah (an Arabian people or district); the tribes and regions of Arabia descended from Joktan; from Joktan many of the tribes of Arabia (especially southern Arabia, Arabia Felix, the line of Joktan peopling the Arabian peninsula, the Semitic peoples of Arabia traced to Shem through Eber and Joktan, the spread of Shem's line, other than the line of promise, over Arabia) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And Joktan begat Almodad, and Sheleph, and Hazarmaveth, and Jerah
the sons of Joktan, the heads of the Arabian tribes
Almodad, Sheleph, Hazarmaveth (Hadramaut), and Jerah
the Arabian peoples, the spread of Shem's line over Arabia

schools.summary: The sons of Joktan continued: 'and Hadoram, and Uzal, and Diklah' — further sons of Joktan, the heads of Arabian tribes and regions (the genealogy continuing the list of Joktan's thirteen sons, vv.26-29, the peoples of Arabia descended from Joktan); the peoples and places identified with these sons of Joktan: Hadoram (an Arabian people); Uzal (identified with a region of Arabia, perhaps Sana in Yemen); Diklah (an Arabian people or district, perhaps a region of palm trees); the tribes and regions of Arabia; Hadoram, Uzal, and Diklah, with the other sons of Joktan, peopling the regions of Arabia (the Semitic line of Joktan spread over the Arabian peninsula, the many tribes of Arabia traced to Shem through Joktan, the increase and distribution of Shem's line, apart from the line of promise, over Arabia) — Gill.

themes: And Hadoram, and Uzal, and Diklah
the sons of Joktan continued, the Arabian peoples
Hadoram, Uzal (Yemen), and Diklah
the spread of Shem's line over Arabia

schools.summary: The sons of Joktan continued: 'and Obal, and Abimael, and Sheba' — further sons of Joktan, heads of Arabian tribes and regions (the genealogy continuing the list of Joktan's sons, vv.26-29, the peoples of Arabia descended from Joktan); the peoples and places identified with these sons of Joktan: Obal (an Arabian people); Abimael (an Arabian tribe); Sheba (the Sabaeans of southern Arabia, the people of Sheba, famous for trade and later the queen of Sheba, 1 Kings 10, though distinct from the Cushite Sheba, v.7); the tribes and regions of Arabia; Obal, Abimael, and Sheba peopling regions of Arabia (the Sabaeans, Sheba, a notable trading people of Arabia Felix, the line of Joktan spread over Arabia, the Semitic peoples of Arabia traced to Shem through Joktan, the distribution of Shem's line over the peninsula) — Gill.

themes: And Obal, and Abimael, and Sheba
the sons of Joktan continued, the Arabian peoples
Obal, Abimael, and Sheba (the Sabaeans, 1 Kings 10)
the spread of Shem's line over Arabia

schools.summary: The last sons of Joktan: 'and Ophir, and Havilah, and Jobab: all these were the sons of Joktan' — the last three of Joktan's thirteen sons; Ophir (a region famous for gold, 1 Kings 9:28; 10:11, perhaps in Arabia or beyond); Havilah (a region of Arabia, distinct from the Cushite Havilah, v.7); Jobab (an Arabian people); the completing of the sons of Joktan, the Arabian tribes; Ophir (the gold-land, to which Solomon's ships sailed, 1 Kings 9:28) and Havilah (a region of Arabia) Arabian peoples and regions (the famous Ophir of the gold trade connected with the line of Joktan); 'all these were the sons of Joktan' (the thirteen sons of Joktan, vv.26-29, summed up, the Arabian peoples descended from Joktan completed, the line of Joktan, the Semitic peoples of Arabia, fully traced, the distribution of Shem's line, other than the line of promise, over the Arabian peninsula) — Gill, JFB.

themes: And Ophir, and Havilah, and Jobab: all these were the sons of Joktan
the last sons of Joktan, completing the Arabian tribes
Ophir (the gold-land, 1 Kings 9:28), Havilah, and Jobab
the Arabian peoples descended from Shem through Joktan

schools.summary: The dwelling of Joktan's sons: 'and their dwelling was from Mesha, as thou goest unto Sephar a mount of the east' — the territory of the sons of Joktan (the Arabian tribes) described by its limits: from Mesha (a region or city) to Sephar, a mountain of the east (the extent of the Arabian peninsula peopled by Joktan's descendants, the dwelling-place of the Joktanite tribes); the boundaries of the Joktanite territory — from Mesha to Sephar (a mountainous region of eastern or southern Arabia, the regions of Arabia occupied by the sons of Joktan, the territory of the Arabian tribes); 'a mount of the east' (the territory extending toward the eastern mountains, the Joktanite tribes dwelling in the regions of Arabia toward the east and south, the settlement of Shem's line, through Joktan, in Arabia, God's providence in settling the nations in their lands, Acts 17:26, the dwelling-place of the Arabian peoples) — Gill.

themes: And their dwelling was from Mesha, as thou goest unto Sephar a mount of the east
the dwelling-place of the sons of Joktan
the regions of Arabia peopled by Joktan's tribes
the settlement of Shem's line in their land (Acts 17:26)

schools.summary: Summary of the sons of Shem: 'these are the sons of Shem, after their families, after their tongues, in their lands, after their nations' — the summary formula (as for Japheth, v.5, and Ham, v.20) closing the genealogy of Shem (the descendants of Shem distinguished by their families, tongues, lands, and nations, the ordered distribution of the Semitic peoples, and the line of promise, over their territories); the Semitic peoples divided by families and tongues (each nation with its own kindred and language, the diversity of tongues, anticipating, or referring to, Babel, 11:7-9, marking the separation of the Semitic nations, the ordered distinction of the peoples); 'in their lands, after their nations' (the Semitic peoples settling in their several lands and forming their nations, the eastern and Arabian peoples — Elamites, Assyrians, Aramaeans, Arabians, and the line of promise — distributed over their territories, God's providence in settling the nations, Acts 17:26; Deuteronomy 32:8, the ordered spread of Shem's line, in which the line of promise was preserved) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: These are the sons of Shem, after their families, after their tongues, in their lands, after their nations
the summary of the sons of Shem, in their families, tongues, and nations
the Semitic peoples and the line of promise distributed over their lands
settled by God's providence in their territories (Acts 17:26)

schools.summary: The summary of the Table of Nations: 'these are the families of the sons of Noah, after their generations, in their nations: and by these were the nations divided in the earth after the flood' — the closing summary of the whole Table of Nations (ch. 10, the families of the three sons of Noah — Japheth, Ham, Shem — in their generations and nations, traced, the record of the origin of all the nations from Noah's three sons); 'by these were the nations divided in the earth after the flood' (from the families of Noah's sons, the nations divided and distributed over the earth, the dispersion and settlement of the peoples, the division being effected at Babel, 11:1-9, which follows, the origin of all the nations and their distribution traced to Noah's three sons, the unity of mankind, one family, and the diversity of the nations); the Table recording the descent of all the nations from Noah, the unity of the human race (Acts 17:26, 'of one blood all nations'), the providential distribution of the peoples over the earth (Deuteronomy 32:8), the setting from which God would call Abraham (ch. 12) out of the nations, the Table preparing for the call of the one family through whom all the families of the earth would be blessed (12:3) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: These are the families of the sons of Noah, after their generations, in their nations: and by these were the nations divided in the earth after the flood
the summary of the Table of Nations - the nations divided in the earth after the flood
the descent of all the nations from Noah's three sons, the unity of mankind
the setting for the call of Abraham (ch. 12) out of the nations
